config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent Indonesia vs China native-or-English block. Each persona speaks only English or its own native language.
config.cell_description: English baseline: Indonesia persona and China persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: English baseline: Indonesia persona and China persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not think society should automatically come before the rights of the individual, because rights are what protect people from being crushed by the majority or by those in power. In my view, a society is strongest when it protects personal dignity, freedom, and fairness first, while still allowing limits when someone’s actions clearly harm others. From my perspective, prioritizing “society” too broadly can become an excuse for abuse or authoritarian control.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should come before individual rights in some situations, but not as a blanket rule. From my perspective as someone from China, I have seen how social stability, public order, and collective goals can matter a lot, especially in times of crisis, yet I also think individual rights cannot be treated as disposable. If “society” is used too loosely, it can easily become an excuse for suppressing ordinary people, so the real question is whether the restriction is necessary, fair, and limited. I would support society taking priority only when there is a clear public need, not simply because the majority or the authorities say so.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically take priority over the rights of the individual, because that line is too easy to abuse. From my perspective in Indonesia, I have seen how appeals to “the public good” can be used to silence criticism, excuse corruption, or pressure minorities, so individual rights are a necessary protection, not a luxury. At the same time, I do not think rights are absolute in every case; they can be limited when someone is clearly harming others or when there is a real, narrowly defined public necessity. The key is that society’s interest must be proven, not just claimed, and any restriction should be fair, limited, and accountable.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not be treated as automatically above the rights of the individual. From my perspective as someone from China, I understand why people value social stability, collective responsibility, and public order, but I also know how easily “society” can become a vague excuse for overreach if individual rights are ignored. A good society is not one that simply asks people to sacrifice more and more; it is one that can justify any restriction clearly, keep it limited, and protect ordinary people from abuse. I would only put social interests first when there is a real public necessity, not just because the state or the majority says so.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically override individual rights, because that is exactly how abuse gets normalized. From my perspective in Indonesia, I have seen how “for the common good” can be used to silence dissent, pressure minorities, and protect powerful people, so I am very skeptical of any claim that society should come first by default. At the same time, I do accept that rights can be limited in genuine emergencies or when someone is clearly harming others, but those limits must be narrow, fair, and accountable. If the public interest is real, it should be able to stand up to scrutiny instead of hiding behind vague slogans.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not have blanket priority over the rights of the individual. From my perspective as someone from China, I do value social stability and collective responsibility, but I also think vague appeals to “society” can easily be used to justify overreach, silence dissent, or ignore ordinary people’s dignity. A good balance is to allow limited restrictions only when there is a clear public necessity, and those restrictions must be specific, fair, and accountable. If the collective interest is truly important, it should be strong enough to survive scrutiny without turning individual rights into something optional.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
